askNext: Which model and quantisation are we actually running?
Is local inference solving privacy, latency, cost, or merely setup friction?
Do we need a developer runtime, or do we really need a production serving stack?
ollama.body: Tools like this are why local AI stopped being a niche boast and became normal developer behaviour.
ollama.example: "They rolled the demo on Ollama because the requirement was a private proof of concept, not a treatise on distributed inference."
ollama.misuse: It gets mistaken for a complete production strategy or proof that local inference is automatically secure, cheap, or enterprise-ready. Pulling a model locally is helpful. It does not abolish memory limits, observability, scaling, or the possibility that the chosen model is merely mediocre in private.
ollama.note: The term matters because it lowered the activation energy for local AI so dramatically that many more people could form opinions before involving procurement.
ollama.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means convenient local model packaging for experiments, prototypes, internal tools, and developer workflows. It is especially useful for reducing friction around self-hosted testing, less useful as a substitute for a real serving platform once traffic, governance, or uptime begin to matter.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A much friendlier way to run local models than manually wrestling the stack into existence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A convenient local runtime that makes self-hosted model experiments practical before production complexity catches up.

ollama.warningLabel: Excellent developer ergonomics are not the same thing as fleet management.
ollama.whyExists: Running local models used to feel more like a weekend project than a developer workflow. Ollama exists because many teams wanted a simple way to try local inference, private prototypes, and offline model use without assembling the whole stack by hand.

askNext: What exact meaning of On-device inference is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
on-device-inference.body: On-device inference belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.
on-device-inference.example: "The team said on-device inference as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
on-device-inference.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
on-device-inference.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

on-device-inference.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.
on-device-inference.whyExists: It exists because conversations around NPU need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.

askNext: Which parts of the company are actually automated, and which parts are being silently done by contractors, software, or wishful thinking?
Does the business model scale without a team, or only the demo?
What breaks first when a supposedly solo company meets regulation, churn, or enterprise procurement?
one-person-unicorn.body: As with many AI-era myths, the term reveals something true by exaggerating it until it becomes unusable.
one-person-unicorn.example: "The one-person unicorn thesis sounds marvellous until the founder discovers that support, tax, contracts, and incident response still decline to become vibes."
one-person-unicorn.misuse: The term gets used as if headcount were simply a regrettable inefficiency. It understates the boring things companies are still made of: trust, sales, operations, compliance, customer support, and the general inconvenience of reality needing more than one pair of eyes.
one-person-unicorn.note: The important point is not whether the perfect solo billion-dollar company appears. The important point is that a lot of people now behave as if it is imminent.
one-person-unicorn.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means smaller teams doing more, solo founders getting to revenue faster, and investors testing how much organisational fragility they can rename as leverage.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A company with software margins, heroic narrative density, and almost no payroll.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A real shift toward smaller teams and solo builders, wrapped in a much larger fantasy about how many people organisations require.

one-person-unicorn.warningLabel: Usually a prediction, occasionally a business.
one-person-unicorn.whyExists: It exists because AI has genuinely reduced the cost of coding, design, support, research, and back-office work enough for solo founders to reach farther than they could a few years ago. That makes the old dream of extreme leverage newly discussable and newly distortive.

askNext: What exactly has been released besides the checkpoint?
What restrictions apply to commercial use, redistribution, or modification?
Can the model be audited meaningfully, or only downloaded?
open-weight.body: This is one of the more honest euphemisms in AI. At least it admits there is a difference between access and openness.
open-weight.example: "The vendor called it open weight, which was accurate enough if you ignored the licence, the data secrecy, and the very expensive preferred hosting arrangement."
open-weight.misuse: It is misused whenever "open weight" is presented as equivalent to full transparency, unrestricted reuse, or community governance. A downloadable checkpoint can still arrive with significant legal, technical, and commercial strings attached.
open-weight.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means you can run or fine-tune the model yourself, but you may not know precisely how it was trained, what data shaped it, or what terms govern your most lucrative use case.

translations.label: press-release meaning
translations.text: We are generous enough to share the artefact, though not necessarily the power.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model checkpoint is available, while other parts of the stack or rights remain controlled.

open-weight.warningLabel: Not the same thing as open source.
open-weight.whyExists: The term exists because there is a meaningful difference between sharing a model's weights and sharing the full ingredients behind it. The industry needed language for that middle ground, if only to avoid calling everything open source with a straight face.

askNext: Do you mean the company, the API platform, ChatGPT, or a specific model?
What dependency does the product have on this vendor: model quality, tooling, pricing, or distribution?
What is the fallback plan if the model, terms, or costs change?
openai.body: The clean sentence is: OpenAI is a company; ChatGPT is a product; specific GPT models are models. If a conversation cannot hold those three levels apart, it is not ready for a serious decision.
openai.example: "The board memo said the company was 'using OpenAI', which turned out to mean one customer-service workflow calling a single model through the API with very strict prompt scaffolding."
openai.misuse: People say "OpenAI said this" when they mean ChatGPT produced a reply, or "we use OpenAI" when they actually use a specific API model through a specific product flow. The confusion sounds minor until accuracy, vendor risk, or budgeting starts mattering.
openai.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a commercial vendor relationship. You are choosing a supplier, a set of models, platform terms, product constraints, and an evolving roadmap. The most important question is usually not whether OpenAI exists, but which exact OpenAI product or model you mean.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: A supplier whose capabilities can become product strategy if you are not careful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A commercial platform choice, not a category of intelligence.

openai.warningLabel: Company name. Not a synonym for every OpenAI model or product.
openai.whyExists: The term matters because company, product, and model are constantly collapsed into one another. If you do not separate them, conversations about performance, pricing, safety, procurement, and market position become foggy very quickly.

askNext: What tools does it have access to?
What permissions does it require?
Is it mostly chat, mostly automation, or truly operational?
Where does the human stay in the loop?
openclaw.body: Systems like OpenClaw matter because they make the agent discussion less abstract. Once the assistant lives in Telegram, runs on your own box, and can touch real tools, the architecture stops being philosophical and becomes operational.
openclaw.example: "OpenClaw is what people reach for when they want the AI to stop explaining the inbox and start clearing it."
openclaw.misuse: By treating any autonomous workflow as magic, or by forgetting that giving an AI broad permissions turns "helpful assistant" into "possible incident". The interesting part is not that it can act. The interesting part is how narrowly, visibly, and safely it acts.
openclaw.note: OpenClaw's docs explicitly frame it as open source, hackable, and self-hostable. They also spend a lot of time warning users to start conservatively with permissions, which is a good sign that the authors have met software before.
openclaw.practicalMeaning: A software layer that connects models to tools, interfaces, and actions so that the system can perform useful work across apps and services. In practice it is closer to an orchestration-and-gateway stack than a free-floating digital butler.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A self-hosted orchestration layer that lets an assistant live inside chat surfaces and reach out into tools, files, schedules, and other systems.

openclaw.warningLabel: A broad-permission assistant is only charming right up until it inherits enough access to become a systems problem.
openclaw.whyExists: Because the market got bored of systems that only generate text and started demanding systems that can actually execute tasks.

askNext: Which model provider, permissions, and repo tools does OpenCode have access to?
Does the local workflow improve reviewability, or simply make mistakes arrive faster?
What tasks are safe to delegate, and which still need a human at the keyboard?
opencode.body: The mature argument for OpenCode is not romance about openness. It is the quieter case that developer tooling should remain inspectable when it starts changing the repository.
opencode.example: "OpenCode helped the team move faster, then politely reminded them that open source does not abolish code review."
opencode.misuse: It gets used as proof that openness alone creates reliability. An open agent can still edit the wrong file, misunderstand the task, or spend tokens with the confidence of any other agent wearing better governance.
opencode.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a repo-facing coding agent that can run near a developer's existing workflow, connect to different model providers, and trade polished platform control for flexibility and inspectability.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A coding agent that tries to live where the work already happens.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A flexible agent surface whose usefulness still depends on scope, tests, and review discipline.

opencode.whyExists: It exists because coding agents moved from autocomplete into delegated repo work, and developers wanted tools that were model-flexible, scriptable, local-friendly, and less tied to a single vendor's product surface.

askNext: Which provider-specific behaviours still leak through the abstraction?
Is the router saving real engineering time or merely extending evaluation limbo?
What happens to cost control, observability, and data handling once another layer sits in the middle?
openrouter.body: Routers are most attractive during the phase when the market is still moving and everyone wants optionality without philosophical commitment.
openrouter.example: "They integrated OpenRouter so product could compare five models in a week and postpone commitment with unusual professionalism."
openrouter.misuse: By implying that abstraction removes vendor risk rather than merely relocating it. A router can hide some differences. It cannot abolish model quirks, pricing shocks, policy changes, or provider outages.
openrouter.note: Services like this matter because the provider layer is now messy enough to justify a broker. They also matter because brokers become part of the dependency chain they promise to simplify.
openrouter.practicalMeaning: In practice it means a gateway for multi-model experimentation, centralised billing, fallback logic, and the comforting illusion that indecision has become architecture.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An aggregation layer that makes model switching easier until you care about the details that never quite aggregate.

openrouter.whyExists: Because the model market is fragmented, provider APIs differ, and teams prefer one contract when they are still shopping for capability, price, latency, or fallback options.

askNext: What exact meaning of OpenRouter API is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
openrouter-api.body: OpenRouter API earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
openrouter-api.example: "OpenRouter API came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
openrouter-api.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
openrouter-api.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

openrouter-api.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
openrouter-api.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around OpenRouter often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: What exact meaning of Operating leverage is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
operating-leverage.body: Operating leverage gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around One-Person Unicorn keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
operating-leverage.example: "Once operating leverage entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
operating-leverage.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
operating-leverage.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

operating-leverage.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: What exact meaning of Operational risk is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
operational-risk.body: Operational risk belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.
operational-risk.example: "The operational risk discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
operational-risk.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
operational-risk.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

operational-risk.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
operational-risk.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Human in the Loop are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: What steps are being orchestrated, and which of them actually require a model?
Where does state live between steps?
What happens when a tool call fails, returns nonsense, or arrives too late?
orchestration.body: When people say a system is "agentic", there is often an orchestration story underneath doing most of the useful work. That is not a disappointment. It is how software works.
orchestration.example: "The impressive part was not the model; it was the orchestration layer that knew when to search the docs, when to call billing, and when to stop pretending confidence."
orchestration.misuse: The term gets abused when simple glue code is dressed up as autonomous reasoning. Sometimes orchestration is just sensible application logic wearing a tuxedo because "if statements and APIs" sounded too ordinary for the pitch deck.
orchestration.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a chain of decisions around prompts, routing, tool permissions, schema checks, retrieval steps, model selection, human approvals, and error handling. Good orchestration makes systems reliable. Bad orchestration makes them busy.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: The thing that turns one model call into something sellable.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A coordination layer full of branching logic, safeguards, and operational compromises.

orchestration.whyExists: Modern AI products are rarely a single model call. They are systems. Orchestration exists because useful behaviour often depends on ordering steps, handling failures, and deciding when to ask a model versus when to run conventional software.

askNext: Which parts of this orchestration still earn their keep, and which survive by historical accident?
Can a newcomer explain the main failure paths without interviewing the original builder?
What change would be hardest to make safely because the coordination layer has become too tangled?
orchestration-debt.body: Orchestration debt is what happens when a clever workflow wins too many arguments against simplification. The system still works, but each new improvement must now travel through customs.
orchestration-debt.example: "The workflow was no longer sophisticated. It had simply acquired enough orchestration debt to make every bug look original."
orchestration-debt.misuse: It should not be used to insult every non-trivial workflow. Some orchestration is necessary. It becomes debt when the layering makes failures harder to predict, ownership harder to assign, and changes harder to ship without accidentally summoning three side effects and an incident review.
orchestration-debt.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means duplicated logic, hidden dependencies, branching nobody wants to touch, unclear state transitions, and an architecture whose strongest testing strategy is hoping the person who built it is still employed.

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The coordination layer now costs more to understand and change than the team expected.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Accumulated workflow complexity that keeps producing fresh ways to fail.

orchestration-debt.whyExists: Useful systems rarely stay simple. Teams add retrieval, validators, fallbacks, routing rules, approvals, tool calls, and recovery logic incrementally. The term exists because those sensible additions can eventually produce a coordination layer more fragile and opaque than the problem it was meant to solve.

askNext: Who framed the problem, selected the sources, and wrote the prompt?
Would the conclusion have surprised anyone involved?
Is the system informing the decision or deodorising it?
output-laundering.body: The machine did not remove politics. It merely gave them formatting.
output-laundering.example: "They did not want the model's judgement. They wanted output laundering: a printable reason to do what they had already decided."
output-laundering.misuse: By pretending the model independently discovered the conclusion when the framing, source selection, or prompt design all but guaranteed it.
output-laundering.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a machine-generated report, ranking, or recommendation being used to formalise a decision management already wanted to make.

translations.label: office meaning
translations.text: A way to sound impersonal while preserving all the old preferences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Human judgement passed through a machine to acquire the costume of neutrality.

output-laundering.whyExists: Because organisations enjoy the prestige of algorithmic distance. An answer feels less political, less personal, and less blameworthy once a system is seen to have produced it.
